title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 1)
text:
Program Review: Modern Languages
Principal Preparer: Valerie Roberts
Contributors: Liz Carlyle, Maria Fernandez, Michael Tischler and Jayne Turk
Section I: Description of Program
a. Organization:
Valerie Roberts, Dean of Instruction
Jayne Turk, Communications Instructor
Karen Beyer, part-time Communications Instructor
Theresa Cooper Turner, part-time Communications Instructor
Doran O’Donnell, part-time Communications Instructor
Elizabeth Carlyle, English Instructor
Maria Fernandez, English Instructor
Mike Tischler, English Instructor
Carly Zeller, ASC Faculty Coordinator/ English Instructor
Jim Corcoran, part-time English Instructor
Jennifer Gomes, part-time English Instructor
Sarah Kirby, part-time English Instructor
Caroline Hopper, part-time English Instructor
Matthew Kull, part-time English Instructor
b. Primary purpose: The Modern Languages Program provides training in analytical,
communicative, and writing skills necessary for a college degree and academically
prepares students to transfer to a four-year university to complete a Baccalaureate
degree.
c. Whom we serve:
English: We served a total of 2547 students between 2016-2020: 57% White, 38%
students of color, and 5% undeclared. Data shows that 32% came from local high
schools (829 of 2547 students); data collection is insufficient in this area as 20% is not
collected and we may have more local students than shown here. Our students are 50%
female.
We served 1937 students who learned in traditional classes on campus (not including
video-conferenced classes) in data taken from first Census; additionally, 532 students
learned on our campuses using VC technology. We served 1795 students through
Distance Learning in English from 2016-2020.

-- 1 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 2)
text:
Communication Studies: We served a total of 1682 students between 2016-2020: 57%
White, 40% students of color, and 3% undeclared. Our students are 50% female.
19 sections were offered at night, 19 were offered via video conference, and 7 were
offered online. We served 1352 students who learned in traditional classes on campus
(not including video-conferenced classes) in data taken from first Census; additionally,
170 students learned on our campuses using VC technology. We served 160 students
through online classes. Online classes were first offered in 2018.
d. Services we provide:
English: We provide core written and oral communication skills need to succeed in
college and beyond, as well as program-specific discipline courses. We also provide
extracurricular activities in the form of clubs, such as Creative Writing Club. We teach
noncredit courses for Older Adults (ENGL 0599).
Communication Studies: We provide core written and oral communication skills needed
to succeed in college and beyond, as well as program-specific discipline courses. We also
provide General Education classes in multiple areas.
Graduation: Communication Studies, as a single area major, has the most graduates of
any academic major. 63 of students earned the AA or the AAT in Communication
Studies during this time. English also had 7 number of graduates. However, English is a
significant component of the Liberal Arts and Sciences with Area of Emphasis in
Humanities degree, while Communication Studies offers a couple of classes to meet that
degree. 35 earned the LAS: Humanities degree during this period. Significantly, 179
students earned certificates in Transfer Studies between 2016-2020.
e. Our program’s services are provided through:
☐Non-credit courses
☐Basic Skills courses
☒Degree Applicable courses
☒Transfer courses
☐Vocational courses
☐Counseling / Advising
☐Financial Aid
☐Facilities
☐Administration
f. Curriculum updates:
English: We updated ENGL 1001 per AB 705 requirements and deactivated all courses in
English at the pre-transfer level. We created the co-req ENGL 0999 to support students
in ENGL 1001 if their HS GPA or other needs indicated additional support. We updated
all Literature courses and ENGL 0599 (noncredit Life Story Writing) in 2019. We

-- 2 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 3)
text:
deactivated all other courses no longer being offered in the course schedule including
ENGL 1004 (Technical Writing) which is in our local GE Area 4.
Communication Studies: All of the COMS courses have been updated, although some of
these updates were finalized after the 2020 calendar year. Most of the COMS course
had an ENGL 0900 advisory prerequisite and that was eliminated to align with English
curriculum updates. In light of the pandemic shift to remote learning, Distance
Education options have been added to all COMS courses.

Section II: External Factors with Significant Impact
Budgetary constraints or opportunities:
• The English department was able to use BSSOT funds to create a dedicated computer
lab in McCloud Hall (MC102) for English Composition classes and the new co-requisite
model.
• The ENGL department also played a key role in advocating for the purchase of laptops
for student use at both the Weed and Yreka Academic Success Centers so that ENGL
classes – as well as classes in other disciplines – could be moved to the ASC when the
use of computers would promote student learning and success.
Requirements of four-year institutions
• C-ID alignment of all ADT required courses to facilitate transfer.
Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates
• The English department has increased access to our courses as mandated by AB 705 and
archived courses than we are no longer offering, streamlining offerings in the English
department.
• Non-Institutional regulations, policies, standards, and other mandates
• AB 705 The English department has made curriculum and course offering changes to
meet the requirements of AB 705. Faculty revised ENGL 1001 to increase the unit load
from 3 to 4 units and marginally increased contact hours for all students taking
Freshman Composition. We removed the lab component (.5 unit), and instead of
students transitioning to a lab classroom for one additional hour a week, students now
meet for slightly longer during each class meeting and receive instruction throughout.
With all students now placing directly into ENGL 1001, the additional time allows for

-- 4 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 5)
text:
both more time on task and more scaffolded instruction for all students. We deactivated
ENGL 0900, the remaining pre-transfer level course, and created a new 1-unit co-
requisite course, ENGL 0999, to support those students who have a HS GPA below 2.5 or
lack confidence in their college writing skills.
Development in the field (both current and future)
• The field of Composition is developing innovative new models in order to increase
access and reduce equity gaps for students in English classes.
Other
• COVID-19 impacted all programs across all areas as the campus moved to remote
instruction in Spring 2020.
• The significant changes in the English Department curriculum, particularly in response to
state mandates, has made it difficult to assess what specific impacts of these changes
have made on student success.
Section III: Progress on Outcomes Assessment
1. Summary of Progress:
a) As stated in the “Student Learning Outcomes and Assessment Manual for Faculty”
approved by the Academic Senate, “Course-level outcomes are regularly assessed by
instructors at COS in the process of teaching: an instructor must assess student
learning throughout the course (through tests, quizzes, papers, performances,
presentations, etc.) in order to determine a course grade for a student. The course
grade may be regarded as the student’s aggregate achievement of course-level
outcomes.” Course grades for all students are housed in Banner (and back-up
grades submitted to the Office of Admissions and Records further delineate
students’ grades on individual assignments, tests, papers, etc.).
b) In an effort to promote effective assessment of course-level SLOs in ENGL 1001 and
the General Education program-level outcome for Area 4A: English
Composition/Information Competency, the English Department organized a meeting
of full-time and part-time English Instructors for a grade normalization activity.
2. Improvements made as a result of Outcomes Assessment
a) In an effort to improve SLO achievement and the quality of student learning, the
English Department added one lab hour per week to ENGL 1001 courses. This
additional hour of instruction enabled students to utilize, when appropriate, a
classroom with computers to compose and/or conduct research with the instructor
present so that students could receive immediate guidance and feedback.

-- 5 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 6)
text:
b) The implementation of AB 705 led to the replacement of the lab hour in ENGL 1001
with a lecture hour, and students who need additional assistance may enroll in ENGL
1001 with a one-lecture-unit corequisite course.
3. Assessment plan for the next four years:
a) Pedagogical disagreements between the Academic Senate and the college’s
administration have impacted the implementation of the SLO assessment plan
articulated in the “Student Learning Outcomes and Assessment Manual for Faculty.”
Specifically, the Senate had adopted and attempted to implement “backwards
design” (which appears to be consistent with national trends in outcomes
assessment), while the college’s administration advocates focusing exclusively on
course-level outcomes assessment and attempting to link this “forward” to
program-level outcomes (which does not appear to be consistent with national
trends).
b) Future SLO assessment processes at College of the Siskiyous will be determined by
eLumen. eLumen promotes itself as “a higher education curriculum and assessment
management system that provides insight into each student’s engagement and
progress. It is a planning tool that feeds data from your assessments, action plans
and other activities helping teams work collaboratively through their review cycles.
It is a client focused, innovative tool that allows faculty to do what they do best:
teach.” Please contact eLumen at https://elumenconnect.com/ for further
information about the future of SLO assessment at College of the Siskiyous.
Section IV: Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators
a) English Success and Completion rates
Overall English success rates in Fa 2016 were 58.7%. In Fall 2019 the success rate was
54.9%. The Fall 2015 numbers reflect the population that was eligible under prior
assessment procedures to immediately enter ENGL 1001 and decrease in success rate is
due to all students enrolling in English 1001 as of Fa 2019.
Looking specifically at English 1001:
• Fa 2016: 59% (245 students)
• Fa 2019: 55% (315 students)
The significant number here is that over half of all students entering ENGL 1001 are
successfully passing the English requirements at College of the Siskiyous, as opposed to
students with similar success rates in previous developmental English courses. In
addition, in a comparative study of throughput rates for students who placed in pre-
transfer level English courses in Fall 2015 with students who placed directly into ENGL
1001 in Fall 2019, there were much lower success rates. In Fall 2015, 119 students were

-- 6 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 7)
text:
placed in pre-transfer English courses, and by end of Spring 2016, only 33 students had
successfully completed their first-year English requirement (ENGL 1001) with a
throughput rate of 28%, which is significantly less than the current success rates in Fall
2019.
The average success rate for all English courses between 2016-2020 was 67.23%. The
Department would like to maintain the 67.23% success rate, but strive for 70%, for the
future Program Review Cycle.
The English department course completion rate across the four years was 71.73%. The
highest % was in 17-18 with a completion rate of 75.88% and the lowest completion
rates were in 19-20 (67%), most likely due to the Covid-19 pandemic.
The Department will work to maintain the 71.73%, but with an emphasis on “successful”
completion of courses.
Communication Studies Success and Completion Rates
Overall success rates across this time period have been at 80.53%, with statistically
similar numbers for full-time and part-time faculty. However, full time faculty do show
a retention rate that is 3 full points higher than part-time faculty, measuring at 81% vs.
78%.
Success rates are interesting, in part because they are so varied. The most academically
challenging of the COMS classes is arguably COMS 1100, Public Speaking. It also shows
the greatest success variants. The most successful term was a small summer school
section, taught by the full-time faculty member, with only 12 students—11 of them
passed (91.67%). The least successful term with this class was during the full-time
faculty member’s sabbatical, with classes taught by the sabbatical replacement showing
a success rate of 52.5%. The other items of interest are that the success numbers
consistently go up from every Fall to the following Spring, except for the (pandemic)
Spring of 2020.
National Clearinghouse Data
MLAN has requested 4-year transfer data and information on how students perform
after transferring to the four-year university from the Research Office, but no data was
received.
Cost/Revenue Analysis
In the absence of data to support this conclusion, we believe that MLAN generates
significant positive revenue for the district. For example, according to Datamart, over
170 credit FTE has been generated by PT and FT MLAN faculty during 2019-20. Our area
collectively brings in approximately 1 million dollars per year in revenue.

-- 7 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 8)
text:
b) PT/FT faculty ratio
English:
• In Fa 2016, 15 courses were taught by FT instructors
• In Fa 2016, 13 courses were taught by PT instructors, which is 46% of all courses
taught (28)
• In Fa 2019, only 8 (or 9) courses were taught by FT instructors. Maria Fernandez
assumed 100% reassign to FT DE Coord for 19-20, although in the Fall of 19, she
taught Am Lit 1 (ENGL 2044) on overload.
• In Fa 2019, 15(or 16) courses were taught by PT instructors, which is 65% of all
courses taught (23)
The percentage of PT faculty teaching English courses has risen 19% between Fa 2016
and Fa 2019.
*Numbers in parentheses indicate “unknown” assignment type--this number for 19-20
jumps from 1 in the fall to 17 in the spring-- what is happening in Banner or in the
instruction office to not code assignments correctly?
Communications Studies:

Confounding the difficulty in assigning meaning to this data are several factors. Video-
conferencing sections have separate numbers for each location. In addition to the FT
faculty member teaching 2 summer school courses, she regularly teaches a very small, ½
unit class to accommodate the graduation speaker. She also routinely has Special Studies
students, each of whom get their own section and CRN. Still, more than 67% of sections are
taught by part-time faculty members—more, if the summer, special studies, and overload
classes are included.
Section V: Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research

-- 8 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 9)
text:
1. Quantitative or qualitative measures that gauge effectiveness:
a. Equity data from a comparative study of cohorts completing English requirements,
Fall 2015 and Fall 2019.
b. English courses modality: Online offerings
2. A summary of the results of these measures:
a. In Fall 2019, 68% of white students successfully passed ENGL 1001. For students who
identified as two or more races, 49% of these students successfully completed
English requirements in the first year in Fall 2019, showing disproportionate impact
for this group. The success rate for African American students was 47% (although
the sample numbers are smaller). The data shows the improvement in the success
rates for these groups in Fall 2019 compared to the cohort of incoming students in
Fall 2015 when the majority were placed in pre-transfer level courses (17 out of 20
African American students/ 39 out of 53 students who identified as two or more
races). Throughput for these students placed in pre-transfer level courses was at
10% and 33% respectively.
b. The English department increased from 13 sections in 2016-17 to 21 courses in 19-
20, almost double the online offerings. In Fa 2016, final enrollment in all English
courses was (575)475; In Fa 2019 final enrollment in all English courses was (595)
494. From 2016-2020, the English department served almost as many online
students as students in the classroom in final enrollments: online (1657) and
traditional classroom (1768).
3. What we learned from our evaluation of these measures, and improvements we
implemented or plan to implement:
a. While we have increased access and success rates, particularly for students for two
or more races and African Americans, these groups show disproportionate impact in
successfully completing their English requirements in the first year. Since Fall 2019,
instructors have participated in Equity and Inclusion training. Many of these
students are athletes who are required to be in a noncredit course at the ASC.
English instructors communicate regularly with the ASC Coordinator and coaches on
grade checks and identifying additional supports for students who are not meeting
learning goals (academic and non-academic).
b. There is an increase in course offerings online, and the final enrollment numbers
indicate an increase in enrollments over time--this could be due to the increase in
online courses, which have higher class caps (32) than on-campus courses. The
English department is now serving half of its students online. The workload for
faculty teaching in online writing classes exceeds the workload of faculty teaching in
the classroom, and students may be getting less instructor contact time as a result.

-- 9 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 10)
text:
To increase equity between the two modalities, it would be better to have a similar
class cap in the online course offerings as there is in the traditional classroom (25).
Section VI: Evaluation
Based upon and not repeating the descriptions you provided in Section I and the responses
provided in Sections II – V, please provide an analysis of what is going well and why, and what is
not going well and why, in any of the following areas that are relevant to your program:
• Representativeness of population served
The English department has increased access for all students in English to meet their
requirements in the first year.
• Alternative modes and schedules of delivery (e.g., online, hybrid, early morning, evening
services)
• Partnerships (internal and external)
• Innovation and implementation of best practices
The co-requisite support course is an evolving instructional model and English instructors are
still learning how to best teach and support students. Best practices are continually being
developed, which is why ongoing professional development is critical currently for all
instructors in English, specifically in Reading Apprenticeship and inclusive teaching and learning
practices.
• Efficiency in operations
• Efficiency in resource use
• Staffing
• Participation in shared governance (e.g., do unit members feel they participate
effectively in planning and decision-making?)
• Professional development and training
• Compliance with applicable mandates
Section VII: Vision
Of significance, the Modern Languages Area has generated 2200.07 FTES for College of the
Siskiyous during this program review cycle period. This, despite having only one Full Time
Faculty member in Communication with a full teaching load across the time period. In fact, as
our disciplines span 3-4 General Education areas, and there is a shortage of qualified Part-Time
Faculty members, we should be hiring 3 additional Full-Time Faculty Members: 1 each in
Communication, English, and Spanish.

-- 10 of 12 --

title: MLAN Comprehensive Program Review 2020 -21 Final (Part 11)
text:
In addition to more FT faculty in our area, we continue to envision a dedicated MLAN building
that is worthy of such high FTES production, with state-of-the-art technology and seating for all
the students whom we serve. This building should include study rooms, a video production
facility, and space for the Peer Mentor program to support student learning.
Computer Lab space will also be important in this new building, with hardwired stations and a
printing area, as exist currently in McCloud Hall 112.
Section VIII: Progress on Prior Goals
This is our first comprehensive review under the new Program Review process.
Section IX: Four Year-Action Plan and CQIP
• Maintain our success and completion rates over the next 4-year cycle.
• All current FT faculty members will be tenured by the next program review cycle and/or
we will be on-boarding 3 new FT faculty members in this area.
• Training and professional development implemented specific to the co-requisite model
for English 1001.
• Secure an ISS for Communication Studies.
• Create and implement a recruitment plan for English and Communications Studies
majors.
• Increase number of OEI aligned courses.
CQIPs will be developed as needed to achieve the outcomes listed above.

-- 11 of 12 --
